video_title: Why AI Labs With Unlimited GPUs Still Fail — Anjney Midha, AMP
gist: Anjney Midha, founder of Amp and former a16z partner, argues that the true bottleneck at AI labs is culture rather than capital or compute, and frames Amp as the 'independent system operator' of a compute grid analogous to PJM for electricity — pooling 1.3 GW of contracted demand across labs and clouds to eliminate stranded capacity. He shares pointed views on GPU utilization waste, the regulatory exposure of neo-cloud providers, DeepMind's research hoarding, Anthropic's coding breakthrough, and why research-trained scientists make better CEOs than operators.

insights.claim: Industry-standard node utilization should be 95%+ (Google treats 96% as near-outage), but most frontier AI lab clusters run materially below that, representing large economic waste.
insights.speaker: Anjney Midha
insights.so_what: Investors in AI infrastructure and cloud providers should demand utilization metrics; systematic under-utilization suggests operators are over-provisioning and margin compression is hidden.
insights.quote: 95% at Google, which is where my co-founder Seb came from — he built the Borg X Borg GQM scheduler at Google. And there I think 95% is considered an outage. So, 96% node utilization should be standard.

insights.claim: Best-in-class MFU (Model FLOP Utilization) for AI training today is 60–70%; most frontier labs are not reaching this benchmark.
insights.speaker: Anjney Midha
insights.so_what: MFU is an underused public KPI; a lab or cloud running at 40–50% MFU is leaving 30–40% of its compute budget on the table — a margin and speed-to-capability disadvantage.
insights.quote: MFU utilization should be — I would say the best in class today — somewhere between 60 and 70%.

insights.claim: Waste at scale in AI clusters compounds quickly because teams initialize plans without iterative bring-ups, and the chain from capital to cluster management to output measurement is too long to maintain alignment.
insights.speaker: Anjney Midha
insights.so_what: Labs or investors that skip iterative cluster bring-ups in favor of big-bang deployments are systematically destroying value; a meaningful fraction of capex in the current investment cycle may be ineffective.

insights.claim: Amp has contracted roughly 1.3 gigawatts of compute demand across its portfolio — equivalent to approximately $40 billion in annualized cloud spend — and projects needing 6 GW over 4 years to sustain frontier research across all its portfolio companies.
insights.speaker: Anjney Midha
insights.so_what: 1.3 GW of contracted AI compute demand from a single entity is a credible price-setter and negotiating block; this scale rivals or exceeds many hyperscaler AI reservation tranches and signals how concentrated demand is becoming.
insights.quote: We haven't secured all of it. That's how much demand we have started to secure… For spike capacity, right now my estimate is we need roughly 6 gigawatts over the next 4 years.

insights.claim: Within the last 6 weeks prior to recording, compute supply that Amp expected to have as excess capacity by end of year has been fully absorbed by demand, with well-funded SF founders texting asking for 15 nodes.
insights.speaker: Anjney Midha
insights.so_what: Spot and reserved GPU pricing is likely to remain elevated through at least year-end; cloud providers and GPU holders are in a strong pricing position.
insights.quote: The last 6 weeks… we thought we'd have a bunch of excess capacity by the end of this year. It's all gone.

themes.theme: Amp as an Independent System Operator (ISO) for Compute

insights.claim: Amp's business model is explicitly modeled on PJM Interconnection — an independent system operator (ISO) that pools compute supply and demand across multiple clouds and silicon types rather than owning its own assets.
insights.speaker: Anjney Midha
insights.so_what: If the ISO model for compute succeeds, it undercuts the case for captive vertical integration (OpenAI, xAI) and creates a neutral utility-like layer that could capture margin from both cloud providers and AI labs.
insights.quote: We see ourselves as what's called an independent system operator. So, if you study the history of the electric grid… the most enduring ones were those that never owned their own assets.

insights.claim: Amp describes neo-cloud providers (e.g. CoreWeave, Lambda) as demand off-takers or suppliers on its grid, not peers — implying neo-clouds are one abstraction layer below Amp in the value chain.
insights.speaker: Anjney Midha
insights.so_what: Amp's framing, if it gains traction, would reposition neo-clouds as commodity providers rather than infrastructure platforms — compressing their long-term multiple.
insights.quote: Neo-clouds are suppliers… they are demand like sort of off-takers of the grid. We see ourselves as an independent system operator.

insights.claim: The historical precedent from electric grids is that the most enduring grid operators started with long-term anchor tenants whose demand was uncorrelated (e.g. a steel factory and a shoe mill sharing a generator), enabling peak utilization scheduling — Amp replicates this with AI labs having offset compute spikes.
insights.speaker: Anjney Midha
insights.so_what: Amp's competitive moat depends on diversifying its portfolio of AI lab tenants across research domains to create genuinely uncorrelated demand profiles — concentration risk in similar workload types would undermine the pooling thesis.

insights.claim: Amp's foundational scheduling technology is the same Borg/GQM interruptible-demand system co-founders built at Google, which uses dynamic credit-based prioritization to allow job interruption and spike capacity sharing.
insights.speaker: Anjney Midha
insights.so_what: The scheduling IP gives Amp a credible technical starting point for the ISO model; similar systems at Google enabled efficient utilization at hyperscale.

insights.claim: Amp is structured as a holdings company (Amp Holdings) with an infrastructure business (the compute grid) and a venture capital arm called Foundry that incubates and invests in frontier AI labs, having put several hundred million dollars into Anthropic from its fund.
insights.speaker: Anjney Midha
insights.so_what: Amp's Alphabet-like structure means grid infrastructure and venture returns are bundled — an investor in Amp is getting blended exposure to both infrastructure economics and early-stage AI upside, with potential conflicts of interest between grid operator neutrality and portfolio favoritism.

insights.claim: Amp is pursuing a vision of compute fungibility analogous to electricity — 'making megaflops flow like megawatts' — including multi-cloud, multi-silicon pooling, with a longer-term aspiration toward an open protocol that SF Compute and others could plug into as demand or supply.
insights.speaker: Anjney Midha
insights.so_what: If compute fungibility becomes a reality (through standards or a new capability layer), it would commoditize cloud-specific lock-in and fundamentally reprice hyperscaler AI infrastructure margins.
insights.quote: The goal is to try to make megaflops flow like megawatts.

themes.theme: Regulatory & Community Risk for Data Centers

insights.claim: Up to 20% of US data centers under development in 2026 are reportedly at risk of not receiving the community support needed to come online, due to permitting, power grid, and environmental concerns — though Midha acknowledged the number may be somewhat overstated.
insights.speaker: Anjney Midha
insights.so_what: Community permitting risk is an underpriced tail risk for hyperscaler and neo-cloud capex plans; data center REITs and infrastructure operators with unresolved community opposition face project delays.
insights.quote: Up to 20% of all data centers this year in the US, my understanding is are at risk… of not getting the community support they need to get brought up.

insights.claim: Scott Nolan (founder of General Matter) proposed that data centers charge an incremental $0.50/hour on top of ~$4/hour compute costs and distribute that directly to host communities as cash — Midha says he would pay it because it reduces reliability risk.
insights.speaker: Anjney Midha (attributing Scott Nolan)
insights.so_what: This community-revenue-sharing model is a potential policy template that, if adopted, would add ~12.5% to compute costs but could unlock stranded capacity and reduce regulatory delays — a net positive for labs and a cost investors should model.
insights.quote: If you're having to bring up a new data center in a new community, why not just say we're going to charge $4.50 an hour and that marginal increase, we just literally take that and give it to the local community as cash.

insights.claim: Midha explicitly warns that regulators will eventually audit AI infrastructure operators who 'move fast and break things,' and that companies without long track records or proper community engagement will be exposed.
insights.speaker: Anjney Midha
insights.so_what: AI infrastructure players relying on speed over compliance face meaningful regulatory tail risk; preferring data center partners with 20+ year track records is an emerging risk management differentiator.
insights.quote: There will be audits. There will be investigations. And when the regulators come, I don't know when it's going to be. The folks who are moving fast and breaking things in the name of AI progress better be prepared.

insights.claim: Midha argues that traditional, established US data center operators with 20+ year track records (not neo-clouds) are the most reliable compute partners, explicitly contrasting them with neo-cloud providers who 'sponsor happy hours at NeurIPS' but lack infrastructure maturity.
insights.speaker: Anjney Midha
insights.so_what: This signals durable demand for traditional colocation/data center operators (e.g. Equinix, Digital Realty, CyrusOne) over neo-cloud names in an environment of rising regulatory and reliability scrutiny.
insights.quote: There are really good, reliable, trusted data center providers in America who've been around 20-plus years. I love those folks. They know how to run land. They can run power. They have credit histories.

themes.theme: AI Lab Culture, Hardship & the Failure Mode of Over-Capitalized Labs

insights.claim: Many AI labs today have sufficient capital and compute but are failing to ship because of cultural rot — Midha's diagnosis is that culture is not a moat but a fragile garden requiring daily tending through consistent mission-aligned actions.
insights.speaker: Anjney Midha
insights.so_what: Investors evaluating AI lab bets should weight cultural cohesion and founder mission-alignment more heavily than compute or capital position; labs that raised too much too fast without defining a P0 are the highest-risk category.
insights.quote: Culture is not a set of beliefs. It's a set of actions.

insights.claim: Labs that raised too much money too early without being forced to define a singular P0 (primary objective) develop the most brittle cultures and often fail to reach capability takeoff, even when well-resourced.
insights.speaker: Anjney Midha
insights.so_what: The current wave of billion-dollar AI lab fundraises may be creating a cohort of well-funded but culturally fragile organizations — watch for attrition signals as leading indicators of approaching failure.
insights.quote: There's so much money going to these labs, there's no hardship. There's no 21 knows. And that in hindsight was a feature not a bug for Anthropic.

insights.claim: Anthropic's P0 from day one was coding — predicated on the thesis that cracking coding is necessary and sufficient for cracking AGI, because coding is a generally powerful capability that can accelerate all computer work.
insights.speaker: Anjney Midha
insights.so_what: Anthropic's coding focus was a deliberate architectural choice, not a market-fit discovery — this gives it a durable head start that competitors pursuing broader capability targets may struggle to overcome.
insights.quote: Their P0 from day one was coding. The reason mechanism system there was if we crack coding, then we will crack AGI.

insights.claim: Anthropic achieved what Midha calls 'capability takeoff' in October 2025 with the training checkpoint that became Claude 3.5/3.7 (released publicly in December 2025), and this was recognized by insiders in the community as a phase transition, not an incremental improvement.
insights.speaker: Anjney Midha
insights.so_what: This suggests Anthropic's coding dominance is rooted in a specific training run that represents a qualitative threshold — rivals need to identify and replicate the conditions of that run, not merely scale compute.
insights.quote: Anthropic basically achieved takeoff in October of last year. That training run… to those of us in the community, especially once post training was done and it was released in December.

insights.claim: Anthropic's capital efficiency has been dramatically superior to OpenAI's — Midha says the gap is 'not even close' — because early fundraising difficulty forced extreme resource discipline that compounded into a performance and culture advantage.
insights.speaker: Anjney Midha
insights.so_what: Anthropic's burn rate efficiency relative to OpenAI is a publicly trackable signal; if true, Anthropic's path to sustainable unit economics is materially shorter than consensus estimates.
insights.quote: It's not even… yeah. But it's so clear, right? Like how to output max for the world. They have been prepared.

insights.claim: Midha frames Anthropic's safety culture as a compounding competitive moat — not because of any one decision, but because the daily reiteration of safety as a value created organizational memory, external accountability, and legal protection that competitors cannot easily replicate.
insights.speaker: Anjney Midha
insights.so_what: Anthropic's safety brand may translate into enterprise procurement preference as AI liability becomes a legal concern — a durable revenue moat that pure-capability labs lack.
insights.quote: How do you prevent against that? Well, day in day out, you say safety, safety, safety, safety. And when you start deviating from that, you have the team hold you accountable.

themes.theme: DeepMind / Google Research Hoarding & Market Failure

insights.claim: DeepMind operates a 6-month publication embargo where any paper that a business team flags as commercially interesting is suppressed indefinitely — resulting in adverse selection in published research (only work deemed commercially worthless is published).
insights.speaker: Anjney Midha
insights.so_what: DeepMind papers at NeurIPS/ICLR are not representative of its best work; investors relying on academic output to gauge DeepMind's capabilities are systematically underestimating its frontier position — and simultaneously, the commercially valuable research is locked away from the broader ecosystem.
insights.quote: The stuff that gets published is the stuff that's not good enough. So there's an adverse selection problem, basically.

insights.claim: Midha views DeepMind's research hoarding as a negative externality and market failure — extraordinary researchers produce work that 'never sees the light of day,' and Amp's Foundry arm exists partly to unlock this trapped research by spinning out misaligned researchers.
insights.speaker: Anjney Midha
insights.so_what: There is a structural pipeline of research talent exiting DeepMind and similar labs (Gnomics example) that represents investable deal flow for specialized AI spinout investors; Amp/Foundry is systematically targeting this flow.
insights.quote: How much extraordinary research has happened inside of DeepMind over the last 10 years… so much of that has never seen the light of day.

insights.claim: Google's internal credit-based compute scheduling (Borg/Brain Marketplace) led to Google missing GPT-3/ChatGPT — because centralized command systems are sometimes required for breakthrough bets that a credit market would underfund.
insights.speaker: Anjney Midha (contextualizing with interviewer)
insights.so_what: Distributed compute scheduling markets are powerful for utilization but can fail to fund long-shot bets that require concentrated resource commitment — a fundamental tension in any ISO-style compute grid model, including Amp's.

insights.claim: Matrox (chip startup, CEO Reiner Pope, former Google Palm co-founder) chose to build its chips to Nvidia's reference architecture and rack form factor, deliberately piggybacking on Jensen's published standard rather than innovating on data center design.
insights.speaker: Anjney Midha
insights.so_what: Nvidia's reference architecture is becoming an industry standard — analogous to x86 for servers — and companies building to that spec reduce deployment friction; investors in alternative silicon should value standards-compatibility as a key diligence criterion.
insights.quote: They picked the Nvidia reference architecture. So, the Matrox chips just plug in to any site that has an Nvidia bring-up plan.

insights.claim: Matrox's primary innovation is systems co-design (logic die optimization), not data center or interconnect design — and its core risk is the trust boundary problem: outside Google, they no longer have early access to next-generation model architectures, which are needed 2 years ahead of tape-out.
insights.speaker: Anjney Midha
insights.so_what: For alternative chip startups, access to frontier model architecture roadmaps is as valuable as engineering talent; Amp's role brokering this trust relationship (between labs and chip teams) is a potential durable competitive advantage.
insights.quote: To do co-design well you need visibility into the next model generation as soon as possible cuz it takes two years to tape out.

insights.claim: Midha sees no competitive conflict between Nvidia and alternative chip makers like Matrox because compute demand is so far ahead of Nvidia's ability to supply production capacity — the market needs more chips, not displacement.
insights.speaker: Anjney Midha
insights.so_what: This is a demand-abundance framing that justifies premium valuations across the silicon supply chain for the near term; however, it is precisely the framing that collapses in a demand correction.
insights.quote: I think Nvidia's not able to meet the demand of production. So we just need more chips.

insights.claim: SF Compute's effort to create standardized futures contracts for compute is something Midha wants to accelerate — he envisions SF Compute plugging into Amp's grid as a two-way demand/supply protocol.
insights.speaker: Anjney Midha
insights.so_what: Compute futures contracts (if bootstrapped) would create price discovery and hedging instruments for AI infrastructure — a new financial product category relevant to infrastructure investors and AI labs managing cost volatility.

insights.claim: Midha argues that top academic researchers (e.g. Anastasios Angelopoulos of LM Arena/lmsys, Robin Rombach of Black Forest Labs, Guillaume Lample of Mistral) make better AI company CEOs than professional operators, because the discipline of competitive academic publishing requires the same performance intensity as great leadership.
insights.speaker: Anjney Midha
insights.so_what: Investors systematically discount researcher-CEOs — Midha explicitly calls this out — suggesting a valuation arbitrage in AI spinouts led by credentialed scientists who VCs mislabel as 'just researchers.'
insights.quote: I would just fund researchers all day… If they've put Soda out there, they're star athletes already.

insights.claim: Dario Amodei's trajectory from scientist to CEO of a company approaching a trillion-dollar valuation in 4 years is Midha's primary counter-example to the 'researchers can't be CEOs' heuristic.
insights.speaker: Anjney Midha
insights.so_what: Dario's arc is becoming the reference benchmark for evaluating scientist-founders in AI; comps based on operator-CEO archetypes will systematically undervalue this cohort.
insights.quote: Have you met Dario? Dario is a scientist. He's gone from zero to like what will soon be a trillion-dollar company in 4 years.

insights.claim: VCs systematically fail to evaluate founders as 'dynamic agents' — they put researchers in a fixed box and miss compounding, multidimensional individuals who are already both publishing and building products.
insights.speaker: Anjney Midha
insights.so_what: This suggests the best AI investment opportunities are being systematically passed by by consensus VCs — contrarian early access to researcher-led labs is likely underpriced.
insights.quote: What I've realized is venture capitalists suck at seeing human beings as dynamic agents. Where they want to put you in a box.

insights.claim: The critical failure mode of researcher-CEOs who don't want to be CEOs is that they primarily want to publish — those who want to keep publishing but are nominally running a company are a poor bet.
insights.speaker: Anjney Midha
insights.so_what: The key diligence question for researcher-CEO bets is: are they willing to be confrontational outside scientific discourse (with team, hiring, customers)? Publication appetite is a leading negative indicator.
insights.quote: If you want to be a CEO, what you need to be willing to do is be super confrontational… to your own team, hiring, recruiting, customers.

insights.claim: Periodic Labs (Amp's portfolio, hosted at interview site) is focused on AI-driven superconductor discovery — specifically room-temperature superconductors — which Midha believes would enable 'flying cars within a few years' and unlock lossless energy transmission.
insights.speaker: Anjney Midha
insights.so_what: Periodic is a high-optionality deep science bet; room-temperature superconductor discovery would be a civilization-level infrastructure unlock — worth monitoring for experimental results.

insights.claim: Periodic Labs recently had a technical breakthrough creating a 'soda system' approximately 12 months after founding, despite not being popular with potential recruits early on — some of whom left for higher-paying jobs and then tried to return after the breakthrough, only to be rejected.
insights.speaker: Anjney Midha
insights.so_what: A technical inflection at Periodic warrants attention; if superconductor-adjacent results are reproducible, this is a potentially investable milestone.

insights.claim: Midha is actively developing an end-of-life prediction AI company as his next incubation — targeting the 30%+ of Medicare/Medicaid spend on end-of-life care, using RL-based models trained on longitudinal patient data (e.g. Stanford's STRIDE dataset of 12M+ patient lives).
insights.speaker: Anjney Midha
insights.so_what: End-of-life prediction AI targeting Medicare/Medicaid spend is a potentially massive healthcare AI opportunity; the main bottleneck is regulatory (liability assignment for AI clinical decisions), not technical.
insights.quote: Over 30% of all Medicare Medicaid spend, at least at that time, was spent on end-of-life care.

insights.claim: The core technical barrier to deploying end-of-life prediction AI is not model accuracy (even simple regression models work well on large longitudinal datasets) but regulatory: US law cannot currently shift medical malpractice liability from physician to AI system.
insights.speaker: Anjney Midha
insights.so_what: Healthcare AI investors should track regulatory reform on liability assignment as the unlock event for clinical decision support AI — model performance is not the gating factor.
insights.quote: You actually can't shift the burden of the wrong clinical diagnosis from the physician to the AI system.
